name: New York
regulatoryOwner: New York State Department of Environmental Conservation
delegatedLocalAuthorities: Some counties and local authorities may materially change the process, so state guidance is the floor rather than the whole answer.
buyerSellerSummary: A sale-side tank question in New York usually starts with homeowner responsibility, local authority differences, and proof of closure.
recordLookupSummary: Look for local permits, old heating records, and any closure or cleanup file before treating a quiet file as a clean answer.
sweepFirstSummary: A sweep-first path may keep the transaction focused on evidence when the property history is older than the available record stack.
removalVsAbandonmentSummary: Removal versus abandonment depends on location, local authority overlay, and whether the tank is confirmed or leaking.
leakReportingSummary: Leak concern may trigger a separate response path from ordinary tank location or closure work.
insuranceCleanupSummary: Cleanup and insurance questions may widen once a release is suspected, especially where local authority requirements differ.
quickAnswer: In New York, a buried tank question often stays unresolved until records, local authority rules, and evidence of tank presence line up.
commonTriggers: A buyer sees old oil infrastructure but no closure paperwork.
The seller cannot show a permit or contractor record.
A local authority may have stricter follow-up than the general state summary suggests.
cautions: Do not assume statewide guidance answers every county or local variation.
Do not treat a quote request as a substitute for confirmation.
Do not treat a missing file as clean proof.
strongestTriggerStates: A local authority or county overlay may matter, but the file does not say which rule set actually applies.
The seller cannot show closure proof and old oil infrastructure still appears during diligence.
The homeowner guide points one way while local permitting or cleanup facts point another.
firstMoves: Verify whether local permitting, fire, or environmental follow-up materially changes the state-level answer before promising a closing plan.
Pull homeowner records, fuel conversion history, and any closure document before asking for disposition quotes.
Use a sweep or locate when the site clues are stronger than the paper file.
documentTargets: Local permit file, fire or building record, and any county-level closure reference.
Old fuel delivery or burner conversion records that date the move away from oil heat.
Contractor invoices, tank test records, or cleanup documents showing whether the tank was closed, removed, or escalated.
escalationSignals: Conflicting local and state signals that mean this is no longer a straight homeowner-guide answer.
Any release language, spill response reference, or remediation paperwork.
A confirmed tank or excavation note that turns the issue from suspicion into a closure or cleanup path.

sourceStack.title: New York DEC Underground Heating Oil Tanks Homeowner Guide
